title: Rocket Lab Craters 27% — The SpaceX IPO Reality Check Is Here
subtitle: RKLB shed $25 billion in market cap in a single week as SpaceX's S-1 filing exposes the brutal math: $68.8 billion for $680 million in revenue and $182 million in losses. The space stock everyone loved just ran into a wall.
summary: Rocket Lab (RKLB) plummeted 26.7% from $150.23 to $110.08 after SpaceX filed its S-1 for IPO, triggering a brutal valuation reality check. At $68.8B market cap on $679.6M revenue (101x P/S) with -$182.6M net income, RKLB's nosebleed multiples face direct comparison to SpaceX's S-1 numbers.

image.caption: Rocket Lab's Electron launches from Mahia Peninsula, New Zealand

bodyHtml:
It took exactly one S-1 filing to vaporize $25 billion in shareholder value.

Rocket Lab (RKLB) just suffered one of the ugliest single-week collapses in the space sector — cratering 26.7% from $150.23 on May 27 to $110.08 by June 5. The 52-week high of $151.00 — touched just days before the rout — now looks like a distant memory. The catalyst wasn't an earnings miss, a launch failure, or a contract loss. It was something far more existential: SpaceX finally filed its S-1 for a record-shattering IPO, and the numbers inside made Rocket Lab's valuation look like science fiction.

RKLB's market cap now sits at $68.78 billion — for a company that generated $679.6 million in trailing revenue and lost $182.6 million doing it. That's a price-to-sales ratio of 101.2x. Even by the loose standards of the space economy, those are numbers that demand a leap of faith the market suddenly isn't willing to make.

▶ Schwab Network — The Big 3: RKLB, LUNR, AVGO
The Big 3: RKLB, LUNR, AVGO — Schwab Network
SpaceX's S-1 didn't just raise the bar for space IPOs — it handed every investor a ruler to measure Rocket Lab against. And the math isn't flattering.
Stock Price$110.08
52-Week High$151.00 (May 27)
52-Week Low$25.24
Market Cap$68.78B
Revenue (TTM)$679.6M
Price-to-Sales101.2x
Net Income-$182.6M
Free Cash Flow-$215.0M
Analyst Target$105.28 (mean)

The Numbers That Broke the Trade

The hard data tells the story. Rocket Lab posted 63.5% year-over-year revenue growth — undeniably impressive, and the kind of topline acceleration most companies would kill for. But everything below the top line raises uncomfortable questions. The company is burning cash at an alarming rate: free cash flow of -$215.0 million, EBITDA of -$164.8 million, and a return on equity of -13.5%. Gross margins sit at 36.6% — not terrible in absolute terms, but far from the 50%+ investors typically demand from companies trading at triple-digit revenue multiples.

The balance sheet offers some comfort: a current ratio of 4.48 and debt-to-equity of just 6.1% mean Rocket Lab isn't going broke tomorrow. With 2,600 employees and 50+ successful launches, the operational track record is real. But the stock carries a beta of 2.5, meaning it's more than twice as volatile as the broader market — and when the narrative shifts, that volatility punishes shareholders with breathtaking speed. The EV/Revenue ratio of 91.9x and price-to-book of 28.0x leave zero room for error.

The SpaceX Factor

SpaceX's S-1 filing changed everything. For years, RKLB bulls could argue that there was no pure-play public comp — that Rocket Lab's valuation was sui generis, justified by scarcity value in a sector with no other publicly-traded pure-play launch company at scale. That argument died the moment SpaceX put its numbers on paper.

SpaceX is targeting a $1.75 trillion valuation with billions in revenue, a profitable Starlink business, and Starship nearing operational capability. Against that backdrop, $68.78 billion for $679.6 million in revenue — with negative earnings and negative free cash flow — becomes a vastly harder sell. The comp math is brutal: investors can now directly compare RKLB's 101x P/S against SpaceX's implied multiples, and the gap demands an explanation that even the most enthusiastic bulls are struggling to deliver.

Adding fuel to the fire: a Rocket Lab director sold $4.94 million in common stock in June 2026. Insider selling at the top is never a good look, especially when it lands within days of the stock hitting its 52-week high of $151.00. The timing couldn't be worse — and the market noticed.

The Bull Case: Growth Is Real

Rocket Lab isn't a fraud — it's a real company with real rockets and real customers. The $1.1 billion backlog, 63.5% revenue growth, and 50+ successful launches aren't imaginary. The Neutron medium-lift rocket is scheduled to debut in 2026, and if it succeeds, Rocket Lab moves from being the king of small launch to competing directly with Falcon 9 on a cost-per-kilogram basis. That's a genuine total addressable market expansion story with credible execution behind it.

Analysts still maintain a consensus Buy rating, though the mean target of $105.28 suggests slight downside even from the beaten-down price. The low debt and healthy liquidity mean dilution risk is manageable. And at 5.8% short interest, the stock isn't a crowded short — this selloff appears to be institutional repositioning, not a targeted attack. If Neutron flies on schedule and the backlog converts, the growth thesis is intact.

The Bear Case: Priced for a Future That Hasn't Arrived

Here's the uncomfortable truth: Rocket Lab is trading at 101x revenue while losing money. Not "investing for growth" losing money — -$182.6 million in net income and -$215 million in free cash flow losing money. At this burn rate, even a healthy balance sheet has a finite runway before the market starts asking about capital raises.

The bull case requires everything to go right: Neutron launches on schedule, the backlog converts at expanding margins, the space economy doubles and doubles again, and SpaceX doesn't eat the entire addressable market. The bear case simply requires that not everything goes perfectly. In a market where the EV/Revenue ratio sits at 91.9x and the price-to-book is 28.0x, even modest disappointment gets punished with the kind of violence we just witnessed.

The Verdict

Rocket Lab is a legitimate space growth story — but it's a growth story wearing a mega-cap valuation. At $68.78 billion, the market is pricing RKLB for a future where Neutron is flying regularly, margins have expanded dramatically, and the company has evolved from a niche small-launch provider into a diversified space infrastructure giant. That future may arrive. But the 26.7% selloff suggests the market just realized it's further away than the stock price implied.

The SpaceX IPO didn't break Rocket Lab's business — it broke the narrative that let a 101x P/S multiple make sense without a direct comparison. Now that comparison exists, and the numbers don't lie. The market is pricing RKLB for a future that hasn't arrived yet. When it does, the believers will be richly rewarded. Until then, the volatility isn't going anywhere.

— The Signal

meta.reasoning: Rocket Lab cratered 26.7% ($150.23 to $110.08) in one week after SpaceX filed its S-1 IPO document. At $68.78B market cap on $679.6M revenue (101x P/S) with -$182.6M net income and -$215M FCF, RKLB's valuation faces direct comparison to SpaceX's S-1 numbers. Director insider selling of $4.94M at the top compounds the pressure. Bull case hinges on $1.1B backlog, 63.5% growth, and Neutron debut. Bear case: 101x P/S for an unprofitable company is bubble territory when a real comp exists.
meta.sourceHint: Price data verified via Yahoo Finance (June 5-7, 2026). Insider selling confirmed via SEC filings. SpaceX S-1 filing context from Reuters/WSJ/Barron's coverage. Analyst consensus from Yahoo Finance.
